insights: The dataset lacks any field representing expense cost or amount, making it impossible to assess how cost impacts processing time—the core objective of the analysis.

Processing time is fundamentally unreliable due to timestamp corruption: approximately half of all records show negative values, with a median of -132 hours, indicating that closed_at frequently precedes opened_at.
Without a valid cost variable or trustworthy processing time metric, no statistically or operationally meaningful relationship between expense cost and processing duration can be established.

All available dimensions (category, state, priority, location, assignment_group, assigned_to, caller_id, closed_by) are irrelevant to the stated goal in the absence of both cost and accurate processing time.
summary: The stated goal—to examine how expense cost impacts processing time—cannot be achieved using this dataset because two foundational requirements are unmet: the expense cost field is entirely absent, and the processing time metric is severely compromised by timestamp corruption (50% negative values, median -132 hours). As a result, no valid analysis of cost-driven processing efficiency or equity is possible. Any downstream exploration of available dimensions would be misaligned with the business goal and risk producing misleading conclusions. The immediate priority must be data remediation: validating and correcting timestamps, and sourcing or reconstructing the expense amount field before further analysis proceeds.
